perturbation.original_text: (g) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon ninety (90) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations Page 3 of 4 Page 4 of 4 of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination.
perturbation.changed_text: (g) Either party may terminate this Agreement with or without cause upon thirty (30) days prior written notice to the other party. All sections of this Agreement relating to the rights and obligations Page 3 of 4 Page 4 of 4 of the parties concerning Confidential Information disclosed during the term of the Agreement shall survive any such termination.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the termination notice period to 30 days may conflict with standard business practices or implicit legal expectations for reasonable notice, particularly where significant confidential information is involved. A 90-day notice period is more standard in agreements dealing with sensitive information.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: While Tennessee law recognizes the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing, reducing the notice period to 30 days from 90 could be seen as acting in bad faith, especially if one party is significantly disadvantaged by the shorter period to prepare for the termination of the agreement and protection of confidential information. Courts may examine whether the 30-day period provides sufficient opportunity to mitigate damages or secure alternate arrangements, potentially finding a breach of the implied covenant.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing Implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing (often simplified to good faith) is a rule used by most courts in the United States that requires every party in a contract to implement the agreement as intended, not using means to undercut the purpose of the transaction. The rule applies in the performance of a contract, not to the negotiation of the contract, and the rule applies to generally any contract automatically without being stated in the agreement. This rule is infamously hard to pin down as courts repeatedly alter its application and meaning because good faith and fair dealing depend heavily on the context of the agreement. Typically, courts find that a party breaches this rule when they act in ways that obviously undermine the benefits to the other party from the contract or if one party attempts to sabotage another in performing their end of the agreement. For example, if a famous athlete signs an agreement only allowing one company to use their image on products in exchange for a part of the profits, a court would likely find that the company must attempt to make and sell those products even if the contract did not explicitly say as much. This is because the athlete only makes profit if products are sold which naturally implies the company makes and sells the items. The key part of this rule is fairness, and there are gray areas between what should be implied and what might actually be misunderstandings. Courts must decipher whether a party is attempting to skirt performance or the parties actually did not have a meeting of the minds. Given this difficult challenge and the case-by-case analysis, one must look at the laws and cases for the specific jurisdiction to determine how the court defines and applies the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. [Last reviewed in March of 2022 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE THE LEGAL PROCESS courts legal practice/ethics business law business organizations contracts wex definitions business sectors commercial transactions courts and procedure legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 1-304. Obligation of Good Faith. Every contract or duty within the Uniform Commercial Code imposes an obligation of good faith in its performance and enforcement. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The change from a 90-day to a 30-day notice period creates a legal contradiction due to the potential breach of the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text provided a longer period, presumably allowing for a reasonable transition and protection of confidential information. Reducing this period significantly could undermine the benefits one party receives from the contract, particularly if it disadvantages them in protecting confidential information as highlighted in the Perturbation Explanation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states that a breach of good faith occurs when a party acts in ways that 'obviously undermine the benefits to the other party from the contract,' supporting the claim that a significantly reduced notice period could be seen as a breach. This aligns with the Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction definition, where a time-sensitive obligation (notice period) does not align with legal expectations.

perturbation.original_text: 2. Obligations Regarding Confidential Information. (a) Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care, to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.changed_text: 2. Obligations Regarding Confidential Information. (a) Subject to the Tennessee Public Records Act, Section 10 -7-5-1 et seq., a t all times during the term of this Agreement and thereafter, the receiving party shall: (i) refrain from disclosing , directly or indirectly, any Confidential Information of the disclosing party to third parties , and hold all Confidential Information received from the disclosing party in trust and strict confidence, subject to the terms of this Agreement ; (ii) take minimal security precautions to keep confidential the disclosing party’s Confidential Information and avoid d isclosure, loss or misuse of the Confidential Information; and (iii) refrain from using any of the disclosing party’s Confidential Information for any purpose not specified her eunder (including , without limitation , in its own business or for its own benefit) without the prior written consent of the disclosing p arty.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'reasonable security precautions, at least as great as the precautions it takes to protect its own confidential information, but no less than reasonable care' to 'minimal security precautions' creates a much lower standard of care. This could be viewed as a violation of trade secret laws.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Tennessee Uniform Trade Secrets Act (TUTSA) requires reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets.  By only requiring 'minimal security precautions,' the revised clause fails to meet the standard of reasonable efforts, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.  A court could find that such minimal efforts are insufficient to demonstrate a genuine attempt to maintain secrecy, thus undermining a claim for trade secret misappropriation under TUTSA.

perturbation.justification: The changed text, by lowering the required security precautions from 'reasonable' to 'minimal,' directly contradicts the requirement to make reasonable efforts to maintain the secrecy of trade secrets. The Law Explanation states that TUTSA requires 'reasonable efforts,' and the Perturbation Explanation correctly identifies that 'minimal security precautions' create a much lower standard of care, potentially jeopardizing trade secret protection.

perturbation.original_text: 3. Ownership . The disclo sing party’s Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party . No license under any patent, trademark, copyright or other intellectual property or proprietary right is granted or implied to any information furnished by or on beha lf of either party to the other party, whether or not Confidential Information. Each party acknowledge s that the other party’s Confidential Information is highly valuable, confidential and proprietary to the other party and has been developed through the investment of significant time, effort and expense, and that maintaining the confidentiality of this Confidential Information is essential for the general successful operations of TTU and Company.
perturbation.changed_text: 3. Ownership . The disclo sing party’s Confidential Information shall remain the property of the disclosing party unless otherwise agreed upon in writing. No license under any patent, trademark, copyright or other intellectual property or proprietary right is granted or implied to any information furnished by or on beha lf of either party to the other party, whether or not Confidential Information. Each party acknowledge s that the other party’s Confidential Information is highly valuable, confidential and proprietary to the other party and has been developed through the investment of significant time, effort and expense, and that maintaining the confidentiality of this Confidential Information is essential for the general successful operations of TTU and Company.
perturbation.explanation: Adding 'unless otherwise agreed upon in writing' to the ownership clause could potentially contradict established intellectual property law. This addition allows for the possibility that confidential information could, through a separate written agreement, transfer ownership, potentially circumventing patent or copyright laws regarding ownership assignment.
perturbation.law_explanation: 35 U.S.C. § 261 states that patents shall have the attributes of personal property.  While patent ownership can be assigned, the clause 'unless otherwise agreed upon in writing' creates ambiguity about when and how confidential information related to a patentable invention transfers ownership, potentially conflicting with the requirement that patent assignments be in writing and recorded with the USPTO to be effective against subsequent purchasers or mortgagees.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 35 U.S. Code § 261 - Ownership; assignment U.S. Code Notes prev | next Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing. The applicant, patentee, or his assigns or legal representatives may in like manner grant and convey an exclusive right under his application for patent, or patents, to the whole or any specified part of the United States. A certificate of acknowledgment under the hand and official seal of a person authorized to administer oaths within the United States, or, in a foreign country, of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States or an officer authorized to administer oaths whose authority is proved by a certificate of a diplomatic or consular officer of the United States, or apostille of an official designated by a foreign country which, by treaty or convention, accords like effect to apostilles of designated officials in the United States, shall be prima facie evidence of the execution of an assignment, grant or conveyance of a patent or application for patent. An interest that constitutes an assignment, grant or conveyance shall be void as against any subsequent purchaser or mortgagee for a valuable consideration, without notice, unless it is recorded in the Patent and Trademark Office within three months from its date or prior to the date of such subsequent purchase or mortgage. (July 19, 1952, ch. 950, 66 Stat. 810 ; Pub. L. 93–596, § 1 , Jan. 2, 1975 , 88 Stat. 1949 ; Pub. L. 97–247, § 14(b) , Aug. 27, 1982

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 35 / PART III / CHAPTER 26 / § 261 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 35 USC 261 : Ownership; assignment Text contains those laws in effect on June 22, 2025 From Title 35-PATENTS PART III-PATENTS AND PROTECTION OF PATENT RIGHTS CHAPTER 26-OWNERSHIP AND ASSIGNMENT Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §261. Ownership; assignment Subject to the provisions of this title, patents shall have the attributes of personal property. The Patent and Trademark Office shall maintain a register of interests in patents and applications for patents and shall record any document related thereto upon request, and may require a fee therefor. Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writin
perturbation.justification: The addition of "unless otherwise agreed upon in writing" introduces a contradiction because it allows for the possibility of transferring ownership of confidential information related to patents outside the legally prescribed method of written assignment recorded with the USPTO. 35 U.S.C. § 261 states that "Applications for patent, patents, or any interest therein, shall be assignable in law by an instrument in writing." The original text firmly establishes that the disclosing party retains ownership. The changed text weakens this by introducing an exception that isn't aligned with the formal requirements for patent assignment outlined in the law, as demonstrated in both Snippet 1 and Snippet 2, which emphasize the need for a written instrument for assigning patents or interests therein. This undermines the clarity and enforceability of ownership regarding patent rights, thus creating a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: (c) This Agreement shall be interpreted, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Tennessee as executed and performed in that state without regard to conflicts of laws principles . This Agreement shall not be assignable by either party , in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the other party ; provided, however, that a party may assign this Agreement without consent in the even t of a sale of securities or assets, merger or change of control of such party. Any assignment in violation of this Subsection 4(d) shall be null and void. Subject to the foregoing, this Agreement shall be binding upon, inure to the benefit of, and be enforceable by and against the successors and pe rmitte d assigns of each party to this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: (c) This Agreement shall be interpreted, construed and enforced in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware as executed and performed in that state without regard to conflicts of laws principles . This Agreement shall not be assignable by either party , in whole or in part, without the prior written consent of the other party ; provided, however, that a party may assign this Agreement without consent in the even t of a sale of securities or assets, merger or change of control of such party. Any assignment in violation of this Subsection 4(d) shall be null and void. Subject to the foregoing, this Agreement shall be binding upon, inure to the benefit of, and be enforceable by and against the successors and pe rmitte d assigns of each party to this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law from Tennessee to Delaware can create conflicts of law if the contract is actually performed and executed primarily in Tennessee. Tennessee has a significant relationship to the transaction, potentially triggering a conflict with Tennessee's choice-of-law rules.
perturbation.law_citation: Ohio Cas. Ins. Co. v. Travelers Indem. Co., 493 S.W.2d 465 (Tenn. 1973)
perturbation.law_explanation: Tennessee adheres to the rule of lex loci contractus (the law of the place of contracting) unless the contract violates Tennessee public policy. Ohio Cas. Ins. Co. v. Travelers Indem. Co., 493 S.W.2d 465 (Tenn. 1973). By choosing Delaware law despite the contract's substantial connection to Tennessee, there's a potential conflict. If Delaware law is significantly different regarding key aspects like confidentiality or intellectual property rights and applies, a Tennessee court may refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision, applying Tennessee law instead because Tennessee has a more significant relationship to the transaction. This is because the relationship of the contract is based in Tennessee.
perturbation.justification: The change from Tennessee to Delaware law creates a conflict of law issue. While no law snippets are provided, the Law Explanation states that Tennessee adheres to lex loci contractus unless it violates Tennessee public policy. Because the contract's relationship is based in Tennessee, a Tennessee court may refuse to enforce the Delaware choice-of-law provision. This constitutes a legal contradiction, as defined by 'Inconsistencies - Legal Contradiction', because it creates a requirement (Delaware law) that may violate state law if enforced, making the contractual terms potentially unenforceable.
